Bitcoin as a decentralized digital curre.canonical: Bitcoin as a decentralized digital currency without intermediaries
Bitcoin as a decentralized digital curre.summary: Bitcoin is a decentralized digital currency introduced in Satoshi Nakamoto’s 2008 white paper as a peer-to-peer electronic cash system. Its core idea is that payments can be sent directly between users without relying on banks or other trusted intermediaries, because transaction validation and issuance are handled collectively by a distributed network using cryptography and a public blockchain.
keyPoints: The original Bitcoin white paper states that it enables online payments to be sent directly from one party to another without going through a financial institution.
Bitcoin.org describes Bitcoin as using peer-to-peer technology to operate with no central authority or banks, with transactions and new issuance managed collectively by the network.

The Cleveland Fed explains that, instead of a central counterparty such as a bank, Bitcoin transactions are verified and cleared by a global network of independent participants commonly called miners.

Congressional Research Service materials describe the Bitcoin network as completely decentralized rather than controlled by a central person or entity, distinguishing it from conventional electronic payment systems.

sources.title: Bitcoin Whitepaper - A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System | Satoshi Nakamoto
Bitcoin: Questions, Answers, and Analysis of

Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash.canonical: Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System
Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash.summary: "Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System" is Satoshi Nakamoto’s whitepaper introducing Bitcoin, dated October 31, 2008. It proposes a decentralized electronic cash system that lets parties transact directly without a financial intermediary, using digital signatures, a timestamped chain of hash-based proof-of-work, and network consensus to prevent double-spending.
keyPoints: The whitepaper’s abstract states that Bitcoin aims to enable online payments to be sent directly from one party to another without going through a financial institution.
It identifies double-spending as the core problem for digital cash and proposes solving it with a peer-to-peer network rather than a trusted central mint or payment processor.
The paper says transactions are grouped into a chain of hash-based proof-of-work, creating a record that cannot be changed without redoing the computational work.

Bitcoin.org hosts the original paper and describes it as the document that first introduced Bitcoin; the Satoshi Nakamoto Institute also preserves the text and metadata, listing the publication date as October 31, 2008.

sources.title: Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System | Satoshi Nakamoto Institute
Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System

Bitcoin creation in 2008 by Satoshi Naka.canonical: Bitcoin creation in 2008 by Satoshi Nakamoto
Bitcoin creation in 2008 by Satoshi Naka.summary: Search results confirm that Bitcoin was introduced by the pseudonymous Satoshi Nakamoto in 2008 through the white paper "Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System," published on October 31, 2008. The available evidence distinguishes Bitcoin’s 2008 conceptual introduction from its operational launch in January 2009, when the genesis block was mined and the network began.
keyPoints: On October 31, 2008, Satoshi Nakamoto published the Bitcoin white paper, which described a peer-to-peer electronic cash system.
Multiple sources distinguish between Bitcoin’s proposal in 2008 and the live network launch on January 3, 2009, when the genesis block was mined.
Satoshi Nakamoto is widely treated as a pseudonym; the real identity remains unverified.
Historical summaries note that Bitcoin emerged during the 2008 financial crisis, providing context for its design as a decentralized alternative to traditional intermediaries.

sources.title: Satoshi Nakamoto publishes a paper introducing Bitcoin | October 31, 2008 | HISTORY
UK high court rules Australian computer scientist is not mysterious bitcoin founder as he claims

Bitcoin blockchain transaction throughpu.summary: Bitcoin’s base-layer blockchain throughput is commonly described as roughly 7 transactions per second, but that figure is an upper-bound estimate rather than a constant real-world rate. It comes from Bitcoin’s design constraints: blocks are produced about every 10 minutes, and transaction capacity depends on the effective block size and the average byte size of transactions. Sources discussing Bitcoin scalability commonly place theoretical on-chain throughput in the roughly 3.3–7 TPS range, with actual throughput varying by transaction mix and network conditions.
keyPoints: Bitcoin’s average block interval is about 600 seconds (10 minutes), which directly limits how often new on-chain capacity becomes available.
Bitcoin scalability references commonly cite pre-SegWit theoretical throughput at about 7 TPS, with broader estimates around 3.3–7 TPS depending on average transaction size.
The practical transaction rate is not fixed: larger or more complex transactions consume more block space, reducing TPS, while smaller transactions and batching can raise effective throughput.
Layer-2 systems and custodial batching can increase effective payment throughput without changing the Bitcoin base layer’s on-chain TPS limit.

sources.title: Bitcoin Speed: 7 Transactions Per Second? - Bitcoin Stack Exchange
What is the maximum transaction throughput of the Bitcoin network? - Bitcoin Stack Exchange
Rapido: A Layer2 Payment System for Decentralized Currencies

Hash-linked immutable chain.summary: A hash-linked immutable chain is a data structure, most commonly used in blockchains, where each block includes a cryptographic hash of the previous block. This creates a tamper-evident sequence: changing data in an earlier block changes its hash and breaks all subsequent links, so altering history requires recomputing later blocks and, in blockchain systems, also overcoming the network’s consensus mechanism.
keyPoints: NIST defines blockchain records as blocks that are cryptographically linked to the previous block, making prior tampering evident after validation and consensus.

The Bitcoin whitepaper describes transactions being timestamped into an ongoing chain of hash-based proof-of-work, so changing a past record requires redoing the proof-of-work for that block and the blocks after it.
A block typically includes the previous block’s hash, a timestamp, and transaction data; in many systems the transaction data is summarized with a Merkle tree root.
Hash-linking alone makes data tamper-evident, but practical immutability in public blockchains comes from combining hash links with consensus rules such as the longest/heaviest valid chain.

Visa transaction throughput.summary: The claim that Visa handles around 24,000 transactions per second is outdated or incomplete. Current and historical Visa materials distinguish between actual transaction volume and network capacity: Visa’s recent corporate materials say its network processes up to 83,000 transaction messages per second worldwide, while official VisaNet documents and annual reports commonly state capacity of more than 65,000 transaction messages per second. Visa’s latest annual reporting also shows average real-world daily transaction counts far below those peak-capacity figures, implying average throughput is much lower than the often-cited 24,000 TPS number.
keyPoints: Visa’s recent corporate article says its network processes up to 83,000 transaction messages per second worldwide, indicating the 24,000 TPS figure is not the current headline capacity claim.
Official VisaNet overview documents and multiple Visa annual reports state VisaNet can process more than 65,000 transaction messages per second.

Visa’s FY2024 fact sheet reports data as of December 31, 2024, and Visa’s latest annual report shows about 901 million transactions per day on average, which is roughly 10,400 transactions per second on average—not peak capacity.

The often-repeated 24,000 TPS figure appears in secondary discussions, but primary Visa sources now describe throughput in terms of transaction messages per second and cite substantially higher capacity figures.

sources.title: Inside Visa’s engine of global commerce | Visa

Blockchain.summary: Blockchain is a type of distributed ledger technology in which transactions are recorded in blocks that are cryptographically linked and replicated across a network of computers. Authoritative sources describe it as collaborative and tamper-resistant: once transactions are validated and added under the network’s consensus rules, changing earlier records becomes difficult because later blocks depend on them.
keyPoints: NIST defines blockchain as a "collaborative, tamper-resistant ledger" that maintains transactional records grouped into blocks, with each block linked to the previous one using a unique identifier derived from prior block data.

NIST’s Blockchain Technology Overview says blockchain lets a community of users record transactions in a shared ledger so that, under normal network operation, published transactions cannot easily be changed.

The NIST CSRC glossary defines blockchain as a distributed digital ledger of cryptographically signed transactions grouped into blocks, with new blocks replicated across copies of the ledger in the network.

The CFTC describes a distributed ledger as a public database that runs on many computers, reinforcing the core idea that blockchain records are maintained across multiple systems rather than by a single central authority.

Bitcoin block reward halving schedule.summary: Bitcoin’s block reward began at 50 BTC per block when the network launched in 2009 and is programmed to halve every 210,000 blocks, which works out to roughly every four years. Verified historical halvings reduced the subsidy to 25 BTC in November 2012, 12.5 BTC in July 2016, 6.25 BTC in May 2020, and 3.125 BTC in April 2024; this schedule continues until the subsidy effectively reaches zero around 2140.
keyPoints: The halving interval is protocol-defined at 210,000 blocks, not a fixed calendar date, so actual halving dates vary slightly even though they occur about every four years.
Verified historical schedule: 50 BTC at launch in 2009; 25 BTC on November 28, 2012; 12.5 BTC on July 9, 2016; 6.25 BTC on May 11, 2020; 3.125 BTC on April 20, 2024.
The reward sequence continues mathematically by halving each era: the next expected subsidy is 1.5625 BTC at the 2028 halving if Bitcoin’s issuance rules remain unchanged.
This issuance schedule is tied to Bitcoin’s capped supply of about 21 million BTC, with sources consistently placing the final subsidy era ending around the year 2140.

sources.title: When Is The Next Bitcoin Halving? [Updated] | CoinMarketCap
Bitcoin's latest 'halving' has arrived. Here's what you need to know
Halving: When bitcoin engineers its own scarcity

Block reward for the winning miner.canonical: Block reward for the winning miner
Block reward for the winning miner.summary: In Bitcoin, the miner who first finds a valid proof-of-work for the next block can broadcast that block to the network and, if the block is accepted as valid, claim the block reward through a special first transaction called the coinbase transaction. That reward consists of newly created bitcoin (the block subsidy) plus any transaction fees included in the block, so the concept is broadly correct but more precise if stated as 'the winning miner receives newly minted bitcoin and transaction fees.'
keyPoints: Satoshi Nakamoto’s white paper states that, by convention, the first transaction in a block creates a new coin owned by the block’s creator, which is the basis of Bitcoin’s mining reward.
Bitcoin’s developer documentation explains that miners receive proceeds from the block reward and transaction fees, and that the reward is paid via the coinbase transaction included in the block.
The block reward is not only newly minted bitcoin: it also includes transaction fees from the transactions confirmed in that block.
Bitcoin’s subsidy halves every 210,000 blocks; after the April 20, 2024 halving at block 840,000, the subsidy became 3.125 BTC, with fees added on top of that total reward.

Ethereum proposal and launch timeline.summary: Ethereum was conceived by Vitalik Buterin in late 2013, when he wrote the initial Ethereum white paper outlining a general-purpose blockchain for decentralized applications and smart contracts. The project was publicly announced in January 2014, developed through 2014 and early 2015, and officially launched as the Ethereum mainnet’s “Frontier” release on July 30, 2015.
keyPoints: Vitalik Buterin said he wrote the initial draft of the Ethereum white paper in November 2013, after months of work on broader blockchain use cases beyond payments.
The Ethereum Foundation’s public announcement of the project was published on January 23, 2014, marking the transition from proposal to open development.
Ethereum’s official whitepaper page describes the paper as being published in 2014 before the network’s 2015 launch, reflecting the public release timeline even though the concept originated in 2013.
Ethereum officially launched on July 30, 2015, when the Frontier release created the genesis block of the Ethereum network.

sources.title: Ethereum: Now Going Public | Ethereum Foundation Blog
History of Ethereum: founder, launch and ownership | ethereum.org

Smart contracts.summary: Smart contracts are programs deployed to a blockchain that automatically execute predefined logic when triggered by transactions or other inputs. Authoritative sources describe them as collections of code and data stored at a blockchain address, executed by network nodes with results recorded on the chain; in practice, platforms such as Ethereum use them to enforce rules and support applications like tokens and decentralized finance.
keyPoints: NIST defines a smart contract as a collection of code and data deployed via cryptographically signed blockchain transactions; it is executed by blockchain nodes, which must derive the same result, and the execution results are recorded on-chain.

Ethereum’s documentation says a smart contract is a program that runs on the Ethereum blockchain, consisting of functions and state at a specific address; users interact with it by sending transactions that call its functions.
Smart contracts are commonly described as self-executing because they can automatically enforce coded rules without a central intermediary once specified conditions are met.

On Ethereum, smart contracts are not user-controlled accounts after deployment; interactions are generally irreversible, and the contract code typically persists unless special upgrade patterns were designed in advance.

Ethereum's energy consumption dropped by.canonical: Ethereum's energy consumption dropped by approximately 99.95% after The Merge
Ethereum's energy consumption dropped by.summary: Verified in official Ethereum documentation: after The Merge on September 15, 2022, Ethereum switched from proof-of-work to proof-of-stake, and ethereum.org repeatedly states this cut network energy consumption by about 99.95%. A newer ethereum.org energy page, citing a Crypto Carbon Ratings Institute (CCRI) study, gives a more precise estimate of more than 99.988% lower annualized electricity use, with current annual consumption around 0.0026 TWh and carbon emissions about 870 tonnes CO2e. So the 99.95% figure is directionally correct and widely used by Ethereum, while newer supporting data suggests the reduction may be even larger.
keyPoints: The Merge was executed on September 15, 2022, completing Ethereum's transition from proof-of-work mining to proof-of-stake validation.
Official ethereum.org Merge pages state Ethereum's energy consumption fell by approximately 99.95% after the upgrade.
Ethereum's energy-consumption page cites CCRI estimates that annualized electricity use fell by more than 99.988%, to about 2,601 MWh per year (0.0026 TWh/yr).
The same CCRI-based ethereum.org page says Ethereum's carbon footprint dropped by about 99.992%, from 11,016,000 tonnes CO2e to roughly 870 tonnes CO2e annually.

Ethereum transitioned from Proof of Work.canonical: Ethereum transitioned from Proof of Work to Proof of Stake in September 2022
Ethereum transitioned from Proof of Work.summary: Ethereum did transition from Proof of Work to Proof of Stake in September 2022 through an upgrade called "The Merge." Search results consistently place the transition on September 15, 2022, when Ethereum Mainnet merged with the Beacon Chain and began using Proof of Stake for consensus.
keyPoints: The Merge completed on September 15, 2022, marking Ethereum’s switch from Proof of Work to Proof of Stake.
Ethereum’s Beacon Chain, launched earlier as the new consensus layer, was combined with Mainnet during The Merge.
Multiple sources report the transition reduced Ethereum’s energy usage by more than 99%.
Academic and news sources describe the event as one of the largest and most significant blockchain consensus transitions to date.

sources.title: Ethereum's Merge Has Finally Been Completed

DeFi total value locked peaked above $18.canonical: DeFi total value locked peaked above $180 billion in late 2021 before falling
DeFi total value locked peaked above $18.summary: Search results broadly support the claim: DeFi total value locked (TVL) reached roughly $176B-$187B in late 2021, with several sources citing a November-December 2021 peak around $180B or slightly higher, and then fell sharply through 2022-2023. The decline is commonly described as a drop to about $72B by June 2022 and roughly $37B-$40B by 2023, reflecting both falling crypto asset prices and DeFi deleveraging.
keyPoints: Multiple sources place DeFi's all-chain TVL peak in late 2021: PANews cites about $176B in November 2021, while ChainCatcher cites $186.8B in December 2021.
An Office of Financial Research report says DeFi asset values rose rapidly through 2021 and then fell to about $72B by June 2022.
Banque de France states DeFi TVL had been divided by more than 4 from its end-2021 peak, shrinking to around $40B by early 2023.
Academic and market sources attribute the drop to the broader crypto market downturn, declining token prices, and severe liquidity contraction/deleveraging.

sources.title: A panoramic interpretation of the current status of DeFi development: Aave and Lido become the highest TVL protocols, and Solana ranks second among the public chains | PANews
Behind the Comprehensive Revival of DeFi: The Resilience and Innovation of New and Old Protocols - ChainCatcher

Uniswap cumulative trading volume since.canonical: Uniswap cumulative trading volume since launch
Uniswap cumulative trading volume since.summary: The claim that Uniswap has processed over $1.8 trillion in trading volume since launch was true around late 2023 to early 2024, but it is now outdated. Search results show Uniswap passed $1 trillion in lifetime volume in May 2022, reached about $1.5 trillion by April 2023, exceeded $2 trillion by early 2024, and more recent 2025 summaries place cumulative volume above $3 trillion.
keyPoints: CoinDesk reported that Uniswap crossed $1 trillion in lifetime trading volume in May 2022.
A 2023 report cited by Cryptonews said Uniswap had processed more than $1.5 trillion in total trading volume by April 2023.
Crypto.news reported Uniswap had exceeded $2 trillion in all-time trading volume roughly five and a half years after launch.
More recent 2025 analytics summaries cited in search results estimate Uniswap's cumulative trading volume above $3 trillion, indicating the '$1.8 trillion since launch' figure is no longer current.

sources.title: DeFi Trading Hub Uniswap Surpasses $1T in Lifetime Volume
DeFi Trading Protocol Uniswap Officially Hit 250 Million Swaps
Uniswap Statistics 2026: What’s Driving DeFi Growth • SQ Magazine

Automated market maker model.summary: An automated market maker (AMM) is a decentralized exchange mechanism that replaces a traditional order book with smart-contract liquidity pools. In the common model popularized by Uniswap, liquidity providers deposit a token pair—typically in equal value—into a pool, and trades are priced automatically by a mathematical invariant such as the constant-product formula x·y = k. LPs receive pool-share tokens or positions and earn a portion of swap fees, while pool prices move as the token reserves change.
keyPoints: AMMs use pooled assets rather than matching buyers and sellers directly; the pool itself is the counterparty to trades.
In classic two-token AMMs such as Uniswap v2-style pools, LPs add both assets of a trading pair, usually at equal value, to create or deepen liquidity.
A widely used pricing rule is the constant-product invariant x·y = k, which means swaps change the reserve ratio and therefore the implied price.
Liquidity providers typically receive a proportional claim on the pool and earn trading fees, but they can face impermanent loss when the relative prices of the paired tokens change.

sources.title: What is Automated Market Maker? AMM Definition, How It Works, Risks | Cube Exchange
What is an Automated Market Maker (AMM)? | A Guide
What Is an Automated Market Maker?

Uniswap launch year.summary: Search results consistently indicate that Uniswap launched in 2018, with multiple sources specifying an initial mainnet launch on November 2, 2018. Uniswap was created by Hayden Adams as an Ethereum-based decentralized exchange protocol using an automated market maker model.
keyPoints: Multiple sources state Uniswap launched in November 2018, with several specifying the exact mainnet launch date as November 2, 2018.
Uniswap was founded by Hayden Adams, who built it on Ethereum as a decentralized exchange for ERC-20 token trading.
Search results from Uniswap-related materials and third-party references agree that Uniswap V1 was the original 2018 release, preceding later versions such as V2 in 2020.
Academic and industry sources describe Uniswap as an early automated market maker (AMM) protocol that became foundational to decentralized finance on Ethereum.

sources.title: UNISWAP: Impermanent Loss and Risk Profile of a Liquidity Provider

SEC classification of most cryptocurrenc.canonical: SEC classification of most cryptocurrencies as securities, with Bitcoin and Ethereum as exceptions
SEC classification of most cryptocurrenc.summary: SEC officials and staff have repeatedly applied the Howey investment-contract framework to conclude that many crypto assets may be securities, especially where purchasers rely on a promoter’s managerial efforts. Bitcoin has long been treated by senior SEC officials as outside that category, and Ether was also publicly described by senior officials as not a security in its sufficiently decentralized form. However, those Bitcoin/Ether statements were largely speeches or staff-level views rather than binding Commission rules, and more recent SEC materials emphasize that the Commission has identified numerous crypto assets as securities while not giving broad advance clearance to other tokens.
keyPoints: The SEC’s 2019 Framework for “Investment Contract” Analysis of Digital Assets says a token may be a security when buyers reasonably expect profits from others’ efforts under Howey, which became the core basis for SEC analysis of crypto assets.

A 2018 SEC speech by William Hinman stated that, in his view, current offers and sales of Ether were not securities transactions because the Ethereum network had become sufficiently decentralized; Bitcoin was likewise treated as lacking a central third party whose efforts drive returns.

Multiple SEC-filed disclosures and correspondence state that Bitcoin and Ethereum were the only crypto assets senior SEC officials had publicly said were unlikely to be securities, while stressing that those statements were not binding Commission policy.

More recent SEC correspondence in February 2025 challenged issuer disclosure that relied on past statements about Ether, noting that the Commission has identified numerous crypto assets as securities and that references to Ether’s status should be revised.

sources.title: Framework for “Investment Contract” Analysis of Digital Assets

Programmatic token sales on exchanges ar.canonical: Programmatic token sales on exchanges are not necessarily securities transactions
Programmatic token sales on exchanges ar.summary: In SEC v. Ripple Labs, Judge Analisa Torres’s July 13, 2023 summary-judgment ruling held that Ripple’s "Programmatic Sales" of XRP on digital asset trading platforms did not, on the facts before the court, constitute offers or sales of investment contracts under Howey. The court distinguished those blind bid/ask exchange transactions from Ripple’s direct institutional sales, which it held were securities transactions. The ruling is important but limited: it was transaction-specific, not a blanket holding that all exchange-traded token sales are non-securities, and later commentary has emphasized that the analysis depends on the economic reality of each transaction.
keyPoints: The court divided Ripple’s conduct into categories and held that Institutional Sales of XRP were investment contracts, while Programmatic Sales on exchanges were not under the specific facts analyzed.

A central reason was that exchange buyers in Ripple’s programmatic sales were generally in blind bid/ask transactions and could not know whether they were buying from Ripple, weakening the link between their payment and reliance on Ripple’s entrepreneurial efforts.
The opinion stated that XRP itself is not inherently a security; instead, the court analyzed whether particular contracts, transactions, or schemes involving XRP satisfied Howey.

Multiple legal analyses note the decision was fact-specific and does not automatically mean all secondary-market or exchange token sales are outside securities laws; application can vary with transaction structure and surrounding facts.

sources.title: Ripple Labs: District Court Holds That Direct Digital Token Sales Constituted Investment Contracts Under Howey, but Other Transactions Did Not | Insights | Skadden, Arps, Slate, Meagher & Flom LLP
Ripple Labs: District Court Holds That Direct Digital Token Sales Constituted Investment Contracts Under Howey, but Other Transactions Did Not (PDF)
SEC v. Ripple Labs: Securities Law Analysis Under Howey Applied On A Transaction-By-Transaction Basis | Fenwick
Changing Tides or a Ripple in Still Water? Examining the SEC v. Ripple Ruling | Ropes & Gray LLP
US Federal Court issues mixed ruling in watershed SEC action on Ripple’s XRP | Norton Rose Fulbright
Ripple-SEC Court Ruling: Implications for the Crypto Industry and Investors | Nasdaq

SEC v. Ripple.summary: In its July 13, 2023 summary judgment ruling in SEC v. Ripple Labs, the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York held that Ripple’s "programmatic sales" of XRP on digital asset exchanges did not constitute investment contracts under the Howey test on the record before it, while Ripple’s direct institutional sales of XRP did. The court’s reasoning turned on the transaction context: exchange buyers in blind bid/ask trades could not know whether they were buying from Ripple, which undermined the SEC’s theory that those buyers were led to expect profits from Ripple’s entrepreneurial and managerial efforts.
keyPoints: Judge Analisa Torres divided the XRP transactions into categories and held that institutional sales violated Securities Act Section 5, but programmatic exchange sales and certain other distributions did not on summary judgment.

The opinion emphasized that programmatic sales were executed as blind bid/ask transactions on trading platforms, so buyers generally could not know whether their money was going to Ripple or another XRP seller.
The ruling did not declare XRP itself categorically not a security in every context; instead, it applied a transaction-specific Howey analysis to different types of XRP sales.

Commentators widely described the decision as significant for crypto markets because it suggested that some secondary-market or exchange-based token sales may be analyzed differently from direct issuer sales, though the ruling was not a blanket win for the industry.

sources.title: Ripple Labs District Court Holds That Direct Digital Tokens Sales Constituted Investment Contracts Under Howey, but Other Sales Did Not
